title: G7 Critical Minerals Ambitions and Iran's Natural Resources
gist: The June 2026 G7 summit produced the first measurable rare earth supply chain target (reduce dependence on any single non-G7 supplier to below 60% by 2030) but failed on a price floor mechanism; simultaneously, a U.S.-Iran peace deal signed at the summit opens Iran's significant copper and mineral assets to potential Western investment, though the dominant investor risk is sanctions snapback on a months-long timeline against decade-long project horizons.

themes.theme: G7 Rare Earth Targets & Price Floor Failure

insights.claim: For the first time, G7 leaders endorsed a measurable supply chain resilience target: reducing dependence on any single non-G7 supplier of rare earths and permanent magnets to below 60% by 2030, with a longer-term objective of 50% — but no price floor mechanism was agreed.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: The 60% threshold is the first hard G7 commitment on supply chain diversification; track it as the policy anchor for rare earth and magnet investments outside China.
insights.quote: reducing dependence on any single non-G7 supplier of rare earths and permanent magnets to below 60 percent by 2030
insights.locator: Q1: What has the G7 agreed on for critical minerals cooperation?

insights.claim: G7 countries failed to agree on a coordinated critical minerals price floor because of unresolved disagreement on who bears the cost — governments face fiscal pressure, while passing costs to end users (e.g. automakers) risks dampening demand and reducing industry competitiveness.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Absent a price floor, junior critical mineral producers outside China remain economically vulnerable; the investment case for ex-China supply chains depends on continued government offtake guarantees rather than market pricing.
insights.quote: A major obstacle to establishing a price floor has been the lack of agreement over how it should be funded
insights.locator: G7 countries have not agreed to a coordinated price floor mechanism

insights.claim: For non-rare earth critical minerals, G7 ministers were merely tasked with establishing dependency-reduction targets and implementation plans by year-end 2026 — no agreed numbers, no committed funding, leaving the bulk of the critical minerals agenda unresolved.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: The G7 framework is far stronger on rare earths than on broader critical minerals (lithium, cobalt, nickel, copper); investors should not extrapolate the rare earth 60% target to other commodities.
insights.locator: For all other critical minerals, relevant ministers have been tasked

themes.theme: China's Rare Earth Dominance — Scale of the Problem

insights.claim: China is responsible for 93% of global permanent magnet production and controls 85–91% of global rare earth refining, with even more pronounced dominance in heavy rare earths (dysprosium, terbium) required for the permanent magnets used in EVs, renewables, and defense systems.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: The G7's 60% target requires building an almost entirely new ex-China refining ecosystem; the refining bottleneck — not mining — is the critical constraint to watch.
insights.quote: China is responsible for 93 percent of permanent magnet production globally
insights.locator: Q2: Is the G7 agreement to limit imports of Chinese rare earths and magnets by 2030 feasible?

insights.claim: Japan has spent over 15 years and significant government capital actively diversifying rare earth supply chains, yet as of 2024 was still the world's largest importer of Chinese rare earth metals — demonstrating that government targets and capital allocations alone cannot build supply chains.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Japan's trajectory is the realistic benchmark for G7 diversification timelines; 2030 targets should be viewed as aspirational milestones rather than committed delivery dates — plan for a longer adjustment window.
insights.quote: Japan has spent over 15 years working to de-risk its supply chains... Japan serves as an important reminder that government targets and capital allocations alone do not build supply chains
insights.locator: The experience of G7 partner Japan is indicative

themes.theme: Western Supply Chain Build-Out: Projects & Pitfalls

insights.claim: USA Rare Earths is expanding from Stillwater, Oklahoma to Goias, Brazil, and Lacq, France, but faces a lawsuit from a competitor for allegedly using stolen proprietary technology and was only selected for DoE pilot-scale testing of its ion exchange separation technology in May 2026 — suggesting commercial-scale validation remains years away.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: USA Rare Earths' legal and technical readiness exposure underscores the risk of relying on unproven startup processors for critical supply chain diversification targets.
insights.quote: the company is already facing a lawsuit from a competitor for allegedly using stolen proprietary technology
insights.locator: USA Rare Earths has publicly announced its intention to expand

insights.claim: Lynas Rare Earths' DoD-funded heavy rare earth processing facility in Seadrift, Texas — awarded Defense Production Act funding in 2021 — was effectively shelved in 2025 after years of permitting troubles and rising costs, illustrating how even well-capitalized producers with government backing fail to meet milestones.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Defense Production Act funding is necessary but not sufficient to build U.S. rare earth refining capacity; permitting delays and cost escalation are structural risks that will limit how many announced projects reach production by 2030.
insights.quote: Lynas announced that the project was effectively shelved in 2025
insights.locator: Even more seasoned producers such as Lynas Rare Earths have encountered challenges

insights.claim: The U.S.-Iran peace agreement's Strait of Hormuz reopening should help reduce sulfur and sulfuric acid prices, which had surged more than 50% and more than doubled respectively since the Iran conflict — an immediate positive for critical mineral production economics since sulfuric acid is a key processing input for lithium, nickel, copper, and rare earths.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Sulfuric acid price normalization is an underappreciated near-term catalyst for critical mineral processor margins; track Strait of Hormuz shipping and sulfur spot prices as leading indicators.
insights.quote: Sulfur prices have risen by more than 50 percent since the outbreak of the Iran conflict, while sulfuric acid prices have more than doubled in some regions
insights.locator: The reopening of the strait should help bring down sulfur

insights.claim: The U.S.-Iran agreement establishes a 60-day framework for a final comprehensive deal, commits the U.S. to sanctions waivers for Iranian oil exports, and includes a $300 billion reconstruction fund; at current production and prices, restored Iranian energy exports could generate more than $60 billion annually for the government.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: A $60B+ annual oil revenue restoration would rapidly improve Iran's fiscal position and attractiveness for foreign investment — watch the 60-day negotiation window as the key gating milestone.
insights.quote: Iranian energy exports could generate more than $60 billion annually, providing a substantial boost to government finances and foreign exchange reserves
insights.locator: Under the peace agreement, Iran will be permitted to resume

themes.theme: Iran as Critical Minerals Investment Opportunity

insights.claim: Iran possesses approximately 2.6 billion metric tons of identified copper reserves — roughly 5% of global known reserves — anchored by the Sarcheshmeh Copper Complex, one of the world's largest open-pit copper mines, making Iran a potentially significant copper supplier if sanctions are eased.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Iran is a tier-1 copper resource jurisdiction currently cut off from global capital; sanctions relief would create a major new supply source in a market where copper demand is being driven by data centers and energy infrastructure.
insights.quote: The country possesses approximately 2.6 billion metric tons of identified copper reserves, equivalent to about 5 percent of global known reserves
insights.locator: Q4: Can mining help drive Iran's economic reintegration

insights.claim: Following the 2016 JCPOA sanctions relief, Glencore and Rio Tinto held discussions with Iran about copper investment, and the Iranian government outlined plans to expand copper concentrate production to 2 million metric tons annually by 2025 — ambitions stalled by reimposed sanctions in 2020 that also targeted 17 additional Iranian mining companies.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Historical engagement from top-tier miners confirms Iran's copper resource quality; if the current agreement holds, expect Glencore, Rio Tinto, and peers to re-enter discussions rapidly.
insights.locator: Following the easing of international sanctions in 2016, Iran sought to attract foreign investment

insights.claim: Iran holds a 15% stake in the Rössing Uranium Mine (acquired 1976); revenues attributable to Iran's stake have reportedly been held in escrow accounts Tehran cannot access under sanctions — sanctions relief would unlock these accumulated funds.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: The Rössing escrow release is a near-term, concrete benefit of sanctions relief for Iran's resource sector; it also signals Iran's existing integration into global critical mineral assets.
insights.quote: revenues attributable to Iran's stake have reportedly been placed in escrow accounts that Tehran has been unable to access
insights.locator: Iran holds a 15 percent stake in the Rössing Uranium Mine

themes.theme: Investor Risks: Snapback & Iran Business Environment

insights.claim: The dominant risk for investors in Iran is sanctions reversal: 'snapback' mechanisms mean the existing sanctions architecture can be rapidly reimposed if Iran violates the agreement — creating a fundamental mismatch between mining/infrastructure project horizons measured in decades and sanctions policy that can change in months.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: The JCPOA's October 2025 termination is the live precedent — investors have already experienced one full cycle of sanctions relief and reimposition; any Iran exposure requires explicit snapback scenario planning.
insights.quote: Mining, energy, and infrastructure projects often require investment horizons measured in decades, yet sanctions policy can change in a matter of months
insights.locator: For investors, this creates a fundamental challenge

insights.claim: Independently of sanctions risk, Iran is characterized by high corruption, stagflation, political violence, and no diplomatic assistance from Western consulates — making it one of the highest-risk investment environments in the world since the 1979 Islamic Revolution.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Investors pricing only sanctions risk underestimate Iran's country risk; operational, governance, and physical security risks are independently significant even under a sanctions-relief scenario.
insights.quote: Iran has been one of the highest-risk investment regions in the world since the 1979 Islamic Revolution
insights.locator: Q5: What risks and challenges remain for investors in Iran?

themes.theme: Trump's New Foreign Policy Model: Resources + Military Leverage

insights.claim: The Trump administration is pioneering a new foreign policy model combining military leverage with economic incentives: following military operations, the U.S. actively encourages Western investment in adversaries' natural resources (Venezuela, Iran) as a geopolitical stabilization mechanism — reversing longstanding economic isolation strategy.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: If this model expands to other sanctioned resource-rich states, it creates a systematic opportunity set in frontier mineral jurisdictions; the Venezuela and Iran cases are the template to watch.
insights.quote: the United States has followed military operations with actively encouraging investment into their natural resources sectors as a means of introducing geopolitical stability
insights.locator: Q6: Is the Trump administration introducing a new model of foreign policy

insights.claim: In Venezuela, the U.S. Treasury has eased sanctions and granted general licenses for Chevron, BP, Eni, and other global energy companies to operate, and has authorized trading in Venezuela-origin gold and minerals — establishing the Venezuela framework as the precedent Iran investors should study.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: The Venezuela sanctions-easing timeline and licensing structure is the operational playbook most likely to be replicated in Iran; investors should benchmark expected access speed and scope against Venezuela's experience.
insights.quote: the U.S Treasury has eased sanctions and granted general licenses for Chevron, BP, Eni, and other global energy companies to operate
insights.locator: In Venezuela, the U.S. Treasury has eased sanctions
